data.title: PCN: The Environment For Bonds Is Breaking Down
data.description:
The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund (NYSE:PCN) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that provides a method through which income-oriented investors can achieve their goals. PIMCO, in general, is known as an asset management company that primarily focuses on bond funds. Bonds are typically considered to be income investments due to the simple fact that they deliver nearly all of their investment returns through the coupon payments that they make to their shareholders, as opposed to consistently increasing in price (as is the case with common stocks). We can see that this is the case by looking at the price and total return performance of the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), which tracks the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade Index, over the past ten years. Here is the chart:

As we can see, the price of the bonds that comprise the index has actually gone down by 10.21% over the past decade. However, it did still manage to deliver a positive total return over the period. This is because the coupon payments that the bonds made were sufficient to completely offset the price decline and still provide a return to investors. Therefore, all of the investment returns provided by corporate investment-grade bonds over the past decade were from the coupons that they paid out. Coupon payments provide income to their owners, so bonds were exclusively an income vehicle over the past ten years.

Therefore, as PIMCO is a bond house, we can expect that its funds will generally be targeted toward investors who are looking for income. Such funds can be expected to boast fairly high yields, and the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is no exception to this. The fund yields an impressive 10.69% at the current price, which is a phenomenal yield for an investment-grade bond fund. We can see here that it is far higher than the major domestic investment-grade bond indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.85%

Markit iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade Index

4.52%

As we can clearly see, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has a yield that is more than double that of either of the major domestic investment-grade bond indices. In fact, it is higher than the 6.67% yield of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK), which tracks domestic junk bonds. As such, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is clearly a more attractive income vehicle than any domestic bond index fund. The yield of this fund is also fairly attractive when compared to that of its peers:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

10.69%

BlackRock Core Bond Trust (BHK)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

8.76%

First Trust Mortgage Income Fund (FMY)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

7.06%

Invesco Bond Fund (VBF)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

5.50%

John Hancock Investors Trust (JHI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

7.05%

Western Asset Investment Grade Income Fund (PAI)

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

5.10%

As we can see, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is the only fund in the peer group that has a yield higher than 10%. This is not surprising, considering that investment-grade bonds in general do not have particularly remarkable yields. After all, as we have already seen, the corporate investment-grade index is barely above a 5% yield and government bonds are even lower than that. In order to provide attractive yields, bond funds need to either use significant amounts of leverage to boost the effective yields that they earn from the assets in their portfolios or engage in fairly high levels of trading activity in order to get gains.

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund employs both methods to boost its yields, but it did not have an especially high turnover rate in its most recent filing period. For the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the PIMCO Income Strategy Fund had a 24% turnover ratio. Its ratio for previous full-year periods was not much higher than that:

FY 2024

FY 2023

Portfolio Turnover Ratio

28%

29%

(Data is for the twelve-month period that ended on June 30 of the respective year.)

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund does not have a very high leverage ratio (0.75% as of March 31, 2025), so it is also not heavily relying on that to boost its yields. Given this, it is somewhat hard to see how it can sustainably maintain such a higher yield than its peers. We should investigate this and have a look at the fund’s finances for insight into exactly what it is doing.

Recent Performance

We most recently discussed the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund on March 10, 2025. There have been a few notable developments in the bond market since that time, and most bond sector movements have an impact on the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund.

One of the biggest, and most jarring, recent events was the downgrading of the United States Treasury by Moody’s. While everybody knew that this was coming, it still preceded a very bad twenty-year U.S. Treasury auction that also caused yields on both the ten-year U.S. Treasury note and the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bond to spike. Business Insider presents this chart of the yield of the U.S. Treasury since the start of April:

At the start of April (April 2), President Trump unveiled a series of sweeping tariffs that would prompt analysts at several major investment banks to predict that the United States would enter a recession before the end of the year. This prompted a flight to safety, which included investors buying U.S. Treasury securities. As we can see here, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note (US10Y) fell in early April from 4.35% on March 27 to as low as 3.94% on April 6:

The recession fears appear to have dissipated somewhat since that time though as the U.S. Administration has lifted the tariffs on many countries that were willing to renegotiate the trade deals that they have with the United States. This reversed the flight to safety and investors pulled money out of U.S. Treasuries and put it into riskier assets such as common stocks. This naturally caused yields to rise as less money was available to purchase bonds.

Yields have continued to rise since then due, at least in part, to the market increasingly questioning the “safe haven” status that has long been associated with U.S. government debt. The Moody’s downgrade means that there is no longer any rating agency that considers U.S. debt to be “risk-free.” While there have been some investors and analysts who have questioned the risk-free status of U.S. debt for a long time, the recent events have made it more real in the eyes of the market, especially international investors. As such, there has been upward pressure on U.S. Treasury yields, and that has pressured bond prices downward (due to the fact that bonds fall in price whenever yields rise).

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund invests primarily in corporate bonds as opposed to U.S. Treasury bonds. However, events that affect the U.S. Treasury market are still important for investors in this fund to monitor because they impact the performance of all bonds. In most cases, when the price of U.S. Treasuries falls, the price of corporate bonds will fall. As we can see above, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note is higher than the 4.19% that it had on March 10, 2025 (the date that my previous article on the fund was published). As such, we can expect the performance of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund to have been somewhat disappointing since our previous discussion on it.

This assumption is correct, as shares of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund are down 6.58% since the last time that we discussed the fund:

As clearly shown, this fund severely underperformed both the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and the domestic investment-grade corporate bond index over the period. This is not surprising given the fund’s use of leverage (leverage increases losses when asset prices decline). It will still likely be very disappointing to investors, as well as a turn-off to potential investors. After all, nobody likes to be holding securities that are declining in price and losing money.

The fund’s investors still took losses during the period, even when we consider the beneficial effects of its very high yield. As shown, on a total return basis, investors in the fund still lost 4.08% over the period:

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund, as well as both bond indices, handed investors a loss over the period. However, the loss from the PIMCO fund was much worse than that of the index.

As I noted in the previous article on the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund, this fund was extremely expensive at the time as it traded at a price that was well above the value of the assets underlying each of the fund’s shares. It appears that at least some of the fund’s disappointing performance was caused by this, as the value of the fund’s assets did not decline to the same extent as the shares. As we can see, the fund’s net asset value only decreased by 2.68% since the date of our last discussion:

This illustrates the dangers inherent in purchasing any closed-end fund at a premium price. Whenever a sell-off eventually ensues, the fund’s shares do not have sufficient value underlying them to arrest the decline. In other words, funds that trade at a premium valuation can end up suffering very large declines whenever a sell-off occurs.

It is worth noting, though, that the underperformance of the fund’s shares relative to its net asset value could be a good thing for new investors. This is because the shares should be trading at a more attractive price today than they were back in March. We will discuss the fund’s valuation in a little bit, but this should mean that potential investors have a more attractive entry price today than they did previously. I will admit, though, that I am hesitant to recommend including bonds in a portfolio at all, given the threats to the U.S. dollar.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund seeks to provide its investors with a high level of current income with an emphasis on preserving its net asset value. That makes a great deal of sense for an investment-grade bond fund, given that investment-grade issuers rarely default on their debt obligations. As we can see here, even the worst investment-grade bonds only have a cumulative default rate of 4.64% or 10.29%, depending on whether we use Moody’s or S&P data:

The average default rate for investment-grade corporate bonds is less than 5%, which is quite respectable. Indeed, most investors will be relatively happy with that, and it is easily possible to effectively diversify that away so that an investor will not lose money over the long term. As we saw in the introduction, bonds deliver essentially all of their investment returns in the form of income to their owners (although it is possible for a bond fund to generate trading profits by exploiting changes in interest rates), so it makes sense that an investment-grade bond fund would advertise itself as providing both current income and capital preservation.

Interestingly, though, the fund’s website makes the following statement (emphasis mine):

The multi-sector fund seeks high current income.

A multi-sector bond fund is something of a different animal. As Morningstar explains:

Between traditional higher-quality core bonds and riskier high-yield corporate-bond funds lie strategies that offer investors a diversified approach to income generation with higher risk-adjusted return potential. Multisector bond funds bridge this gap by investing in a mix of fixed-income sectors (U.S. government, securitized, investment-grade corporates, non-U.S., and high-yield debt).

Morningstar has the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund classified as an investment-grade bond fund, which suggests that it holds primarily U.S. government and investment-grade corporate debt. That would be consistent with the fund’s secondary objective of capital preservation, although it would mean sacrificing a certain amount of income potential. The fund’s portfolio, however, looks like this:

We see that the fund has 38.07% of its assets by market value invested in junk bonds (High Yield Credit) and another 15.36% of its assets invested in emerging markets. That is 53.43% of its assets by market value invested in these two categories, and both categories are generally considered to be higher on the risk scale than investment-grade corporate bonds. This allocation suggests that the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is a multi-sector bond fund and not an investment-grade bond fund. However, it does appear to have a much higher allocation to investment-grade credit than other multi-sector bond funds. For example, the DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund (DSL) has this sector allocation listed on its website:

The DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund has 21.02% of its assets invested in leveraged loans, 7.29% in collateralized debt obligations, 35.90% in emerging markets, and 20.14% in junk bonds. While not all of these are necessarily below-investment-grade credit, the majority of outstanding debt in all four of those categories carries a junk rating. Thus, the DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund appears to have at least 75% to 80% of its assets invested in below-investment-grade credit and may have even more than that. This is much higher than what the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has.

Another way to look at a bond fund’s portfolio exposure is duration-weighted exposure. This basically tells us the extent to which a change in interest rates will affect the fund’s portfolio value. In the case of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund, 49.35% of its duration-weighted exposure comes from U.S. Government-related securities. We can see that another 8.00% of its duration-weighted exposure comes from investment-grade credit, and another 12.09% of its duration-weighted exposure comes from non-agency mortgages. This alone results in well over half of the fund’s duration-weighted exposure coming from investment-grade securities, which is very similar to what an investment-grade bond fund would possess.

We can therefore see that the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is not a pure investment-grade bond fund like some of the others that share the same Morningstar classification. However, it is still far more exposed to investment-grade credit and conditions impacting that market than a multi-sector bond fund would be. As such, we can continue to analyze this fund like an investment-grade bond fund. We should simply keep in mind that it holds several securities with higher yields and potentially higher default risks than most investment-grade bond funds.

Alternative Default Measurements And Concerns

The primary appeal that investment-grade credit has over junk-rated debt is the lower default rate. As we saw earlier, the average cumulative default rate on investment-grade bonds is less than 5%. However, non-investment-grade (junk) debt has an average cumulative default rate of 31.37% to 42.35%, depending on whether we use Moody’s or Standard & Poor’s data. However, this only refers to a particular kind of default – non-payment of principal and interest. There is a second form of default, and this second form of default is one of the biggest reasons why I am hesitant to recommend purchasing any fixed-income security, most especially investment-grade debt.

On Monday, Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, the largest hedge fund manager in the world, said that Moody’s and the other debt-rating agencies are missing a huge risk with U.S. Treasuries. According to Dalio, the credit rating agencies only take into account the risk of outright non-payment of debt, and he is correct. The risk of this kind of default is very low on investment-grade debt. However, bonds only require payment in nominal terms in order to avoid a default, not repayment in real terms. As such, a bond investor can lose purchasing power over the lifetime of a given bond even if the issuer does not default. This is a particularly big problem for investment-grade credit, as the interest rate paid by these securities might not be sufficient to offset the loss of purchasing power.

Former Wall Street analyst and financial author John Rubino has taken this theme even further. In an interview published Friday, he pointed out that the Federal Reserve appears to be losing control of interest rates. The central bank has been promising cuts this year, and the market appears to agree that this is the most likely scenario. However, as we saw earlier in this article, the ten-year U.S. Treasury yield has been rising over the past month or two. The thirty-year bond has actually seen its yield increase year-to-date:

At the start of this year, the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bond had a yield of 4.79%. Today, it yields 5.02%, an increase of 23 basis points year-to-date. This comes despite the fact that the Federal Reserve has been consistently stating that it intends to cut interest rates by 125 basis points by the end of 2027. The only explanation for this is that investors doubt that the United States will be able to repay its debts without resorting to money-printing and devaluing the U.S. dollar. This is the alternative default scenario, in that the debt will be repaid with a currency that is worth far less than was originally lent. Thus, the investor does not avoid losing money, as they could still very well lose purchasing power.

It is worth noting that all U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities have the same problem in this scenario. As this scenario suggests a devaluation of the U.S. dollar, any bond that simply pays back the face amount at maturity will result in the investor getting back a lot less purchasing power than they originally lent.

Over the past year, we have also seen the Japanese government bond yield curve shift upward:

This tells us that the problem is not limited to the United States or to U.S. dollar-denominated bonds. However, it is certainly possible that there may still be ways for a fixed-income investor to preserve their purchasing power by purchasing bonds denominated in certain foreign currencies. However, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has very limited exposure to anything other than U.S. dollar-denominated bonds. Thus, this fund will likely be impacted should the scenario just described come to pass.

Valuation

Shares of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund are currently trading at an 8.60% premium to net asset value. This is better than the 9.61% premium that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but today’s price still represents a premium on net asset value, and anyone who purchases the fund at today’s price is paying more than the underlying assets are actually worth.

The last time that we discussed the fund, it was trading at a whopping 13.71% premium to net asset value. As expected, then, some of the poor performance that this fund has delivered since early March was caused by the premium shrinking and not by the performance of the fund’s underlying assets.

Over the past three years, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has traded at pricing ranging from a 1.32% premium to a 29.00% premium to net asset value. The average price over the period was a 14.25% premium, so we can see that the fund is currently trading at a better price than that. However, it is still very expensive, which we can see by comparing it to its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund

8.60%

BlackRock Core Bond Trust

3.91%

First Trust Mortgage Income Fund

(3.60%)

Invesco Bond Fund

(4.20%)

John Hancock Investors Trust

(8.33%)

Western Asset Investment Grade Income Fund

(3.29%)

As was the case back in March, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is substantially more expensive than any of its peers. The price of this fund has admittedly come down, and a few of the fund’s peers are today more expensive than they were previously, but that has not been enough to close the price gap between this fund and similar closed-end bond funds in the market.

It is hard to justify paying a premium for any bond fund due to the likelihood of a stealth default due to money-printing. The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has not historically traded at a discount, but it has had smaller premiums than currently.

Expenses

The fund’s most recent fact sheet states that the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has a total expense ratio of 2.32%:

The majority of the fund’s expenses come from interest on the money that it has borrowed to obtain leverage, which is not particularly surprising. After all, most funds have leverage-carrying costs accounting for most of their expenses.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the overall environment for bonds and fixed-income securities appears to be deteriorating. There are signs that the Federal Reserve (and a few other central banks) have lost control over the long end of the yield curve, which is probably because investors are beginning to wonder how the governments of various developed countries will be able to afford to carry their current and projected debt loads without resorting to money-printing and currency devaluation.

This, unfortunately, makes it very difficult to recommend any bond fund right now. While the yield of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is quite attractive, anyone who purchases it at the current price is overpaying for the assets that the fund possesses, and overpaying for bonds is very hard to justify.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IGLD: Safe-Haven Value Perspectives
data.description:
Introduction

FT Cboe Vest Gold Strategy Target Income ETF (BATS:IGLD) is a hybrid fund designed for investors seeking exposure to gold and steady cash flows. The fund articulates its performance objective through three clearly differentiated types of operation:

Treasury - 1-month Bills

The fund invests the majority of its assets in very short-term US Treasury securities (T-Bills). These instruments generate a ‘risk-free’ return that serves as a benchmark and provides stability to the vehicle in volatile market environments.

Exposure to the gold price via FLEX options on GLD

Rather than directly purchasing shares of the SPDR Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:GLD), the ETF does so through an affiliate in the Cayman Islands that purchases Flexible Exchange Options (‘FLEX Options’) referenced to GLD. On a monthly basis, the fund purchases longs of options (calls) to replicate 50-100% of the appreciation of GLD, and simultaneously sells options (puts or call spreads) to fund part of that exposure. At each option sale date, there is an ‘Upside Participation %’ that determines how much of the GLD rally the fund captures; it is typically between 60% and 80% depending on premiums and volatility.

Generating premiums to cover the target coupon

By selling these options on GLD, IGLD earns a volatility premium, which is distributed periodically as a coupon to all investors in the fund. The objective is that, when added to the T-bill yield, the total flow should exceed the 1-month bill yield by about 3.85 p.p., before subtracting costs and expenses.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

Liquidity - M2

Next, we will analyze several catalysts that have increased the value of gold in recent times. Then, based on this analysis and looking at the future outlook, we will make a recommendation on whether to buy/sell or hold the vehicle in question.

First, we focus on global liquidity and its relationship with gold. As can be seen in the following graph, in this last period, from December 2024 until today, there has been an increase in liquidity around the globe, mainly due to the powerful injection of liquidity by the Chinese central bank (PBOC) into its economy. All this liquidity injected into the financial system has caused major financial assets to appreciate in their own right.

As a telling point, I believe that this injection of liquidity has only just begun, so it is a factor to be taken into account for the future appreciation of gold. If we also take into account that an increase in M2 (money supply) tends to depress the value of traditional currencies, this favors the rotation of capital flows to safe-haven assets such as gold or bitcoin, which, just by holding them, acts as a hedge against possible monetary inflation (in financial assets) caused by this increase in liquidity.

All of the above can be clearly seen in the years 2020–2021, where gold appreciated by 40% after the intensive QE programs applied by the FED to solve the problems derived from the COVID - 19 pandemic. Moreover, in the case of IGLD, one-month T-Bills, with rates at historically high levels, reinforce the target coupon, providing an income cushion even if gold corrects.

DXY

Secondly, we looked at the performance of the dollar (DXY). As FT Vest IGLD is denominated in USD and unhedged, an appreciation of the dollar directly penalizes both the price of gold and the value of T-Bills when converted into another currency (e.g. euros). It is clear from the chart below that the local peaks of the DXY coincide with the local valleys of gold. During the first part of 2025 the DXY has fallen, which has accompanied a strong rally in gold. Moreover, Washington has incentives to keep the dollar relatively low - given its high trade deficit - which would continue to support gold and, by extension, the vehicle in question.

Central Banks Historical Purchases

In the third point, we will study the different actions of the major market players, such as the central banks. In the following graph, we have a history since 2022 of the changes in the price of gold and the purchases made by the world's main central banks. These central banks accumulate gold as reserves, and we can see how the historical purchases have increased since the aforementioned date. This is a good indicator for all those who are thinking of exposing themselves to the asset through an investment vehicle such as the one analyzed in this article.

VIX & MOVE Index

In recent weeks, both the MOVE Index (fixed income volatility) and the VIX (equity volatility) have seen significant spikes, reflecting the growing uncertainty over the tariff war and nervousness in equity markets. Historically, when these indices reach highs - such as in March 2020 or late 2024 - gold consolidates as a natural safe haven, rising sharply in the face of fears of Fed hikes or stock market corrections.

In this scenario, IGLD offers a unique combination: on the one hand, it participates in the gold rally via FLEX options on GLD; on the other hand, it uses one-month T-Bills to take advantage of high yields on short rates; and, in addition, it sells volatility premiums to finance a target coupon of around 3.8%. Thus, buying IGLD now not only provides protection against extreme volatility, but also captures gold's upside potential while generating an attractive income stream in an environment of macro and geopolitical uncertainty.

Tariffs Agreements

The uncertainty mentioned in the previous section is largely caused by the current tariff situation in the US. While it is true that the Trump administration has reduced its tariffs on Chinese goods from up to 145% to 30% and in return, China will reduce its tariffs on US imports from 125% to 10%, which will remain in place for 90 days to allow for further negotiations to take place, there is still no official announcement of a major lasting agreement. This suggests that it is quite likely that there will again be a lot of volatility in the markets, which we have seen clearly favors gold as it acts as a safe haven asset.

Conclusion

In conclusion and to end this article, we believe that there is a good opportunity to buy the vehicle at the moment, although gold is at record highs, due to the current macroeconomic situation, the more than likely existence of future volatility (higher premiums) during 2025 and the existing geopolitical and commercial tensions. All this added to an increase in reserve accumulation by central banks and a sustained growth in liquidity/M2, we consider gold and the ETF a good investment opportunity at the moment.

data.title: CommScope Holding Stock Is Up On Increasing Q1 Revenues, But Risk Is High
data.description:
CommScope Holding Company (NASDAQ:COMM) reported improved Q1-2025 earnings earlier this month with increasing revenues and net income. The company’s stock price is up 325% over the last twelve months and 40% over the last thirty days. The company manufactures and sells hardware and software for network infrastructure. Its products and services are in high demand with data centers. The company is undervalued compared to its peers, but its high debt to revenue ratio is out of proportion and a high risk. I rate the company as a Hold for now.

Q1-2025 Results

CommScope Holding reported $1.112 billion in revenues, an increase of 23% YoY and a 4.87% decrease QoQ. The company reported gross profits of $468.6 million, an increase of 58.8% YoY and an increase of 4.69% QoQ. Profit margin for Q1 was 42.13% versus 32.75% YoY and 38.28% QoQ.

The company reported a net income of $784.0 million versus a net loss of $333.7 YoY and a net income of $6.7 million QoQ. Market consensus for Q2-2025 revenues is $1.27 billion, representing an increase YoY and QoQ. The company reports in July. CommScope Holding expects higher revenues for Q2.

All operating segments experienced YoY growth. The company’s Connectivity and Cable Solutions (CCS) segment brought in the most revenues, while its Network (NICS) segment saw the largest increase YoY. The company provided the following breakdown of revenues by segment.

Demand from hyperscaler and cloud data centers drove growth in the CCS segment. Enterprise fiber products for data centers rose 88% YoY and contributed $213 million to the total. The company’s SYSTIMAX 2.0 product is part of this mix. The demand comes from data centers updating their network infrastructure to meet the demand of AI computing. Q2 revenue in this segment is expected to increase.

Wi-Fi 7 products and the company’s RUCKUS products are driving growth in its NICS segment. RUCKUS consists of Wi-Fi network solutions including hardware and software. According to the company, NICS segment revenue may decline in Q2.

The company’s ANS segment was driven by sales of DOCSIS 4.0 products and legacy license sales. Revenues in this segment are expected to increase for Q2.

Cash on hand decreased due to the divestment of the company’s OWN (Outdoor Wireless Networks) and DAS (Distributed Antenna Systems) business segments. The company used the proceeds from the sales of these segments to pay down debt and to postpone debt maturities until 2027. Total debt is around $7.2 billion. The company paid it down by about $2 billion with the sales of the segments. The company plans to reduce debt further by 2026.

The company did not report free cash flow for the quarter. It expects to report break even cash flow for physical year 2025. It did report free cash flow for 2024 physical year.

CommScope Holding expects minor issues with tariffs, somewhere between $10 million and $15 million of additional costs for Q2. The company has a large global footprint and sources its raw materials locally. Products for 80% of its US sales were manufactured in the US or a compliant country.

The company has seen an increase in revenues over the last three quarters. The number has surpassed $1 billion per quarter and profit margins have increased. Net income is inconsistent as is free cash flow.

Current assets outweigh current liabilities, but long-term assets are less than long-term liabilities by $1.3 billion. Liabilities have decreased due to debt being paid down. The high debt makes the company’s book value per share and NAV per share value to be negative.

Debt remains high and interest payments take up a large amount of quarterly cash flow. Total debt to EBITDA is 8x; the company expects to bring the ratio of debt to adjusted EBITDA to 6x.

Cisco Systems leads the pack in terms of financial performance. It has $22.8 billion in debt, which is in proportion to its earnings. The company reports net income and free cash flow. Cisco Systems is a bit overvalued compared to its peers. Its market cap is higher than its long-term assets.

NetGear market cap is near its long-term asset value and it trades close to its median price target. The company has improving financial performance. It is undervalued compared to its peers. NetGear has low debt and reports some free cash flow. It often operates at a net loss.

Applied Optoelectronics is overvalued; its market cap is higher than its long-term assets. The company has $46 million in debt and trades below its median target price. The company has good financial performance and its stock has benefited from positive investor sentiment.

AXT is undervalued and has mediocre financial performance. The company operates at a net loss and reports some free cash flow. It has $50 million in short-term debt and $1.7 million in long-term debt. It trades below its book value and median target price.

CommScope Holding is undervalued compared to its peers, but its high debt makes it a risk. The company has excellent financial performance otherwise. It reports net income and some free cash follow. The company’s assets are higher than its market cap. The company’s liabilities outweigh its assets.

CommScope Holding is at low risk of poor financial performance, but a Hold rating for the company comes at a moderate risk to the investor.

Conclusion

CommScope Holding reported improved financial performance for Q1-2025 and expects increased revenues for Q2-2025. The company’s operating segments are increasing due to high demand for its products. The company’s stock price is up significantly over the last twelve months. The company has high debt compared to its income. The company has plans to bring down its debt ratio, but risk abounds for the investor. I rate the company as a Hold for now and recommend that investors watch for future developments from the company.

data.title: JOYY: Deep Value And Amazing Shareholder Returns
data.description:
JOYY Inc. (NASDAQ:JOYY), a video-based social media company, offers a big discount on its net cash pile and generous shareholder returns, despite a stagnant growth outlook. The stock trades at a depressed valuation, partly due to general pessimism about businesses with Chinese origins and links.

Using the dividend discount model (DDM) and assuming zero growth, I value the company at more than twice its current market cap. A similar result can be derived from discounted free cash flows. I’m initiating JOYY coverage with a buy rating and a price target of $89.71, implying a potential upside of more than 100%.

Quick overview

JOYY was founded in 2005 by David Xueling Li, a Chinese journalist-turned-entrepreneur, in South China’s Guangzhou city. The operator of social media platforms has since expanded globally and moved its headquarters to Singapore. Its key brands include live broadcaster Bigo Live, short video platform Likee, social game platform Hago, and instant-messaging app imo.

The company focuses on providing users with real-time “interactive and immersive entertainment”. JOYY derives about one-tenth of its revenue from China, with the rest coming from North America, Europe, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, and other regions, according to the company's 2024 annual report.

Cash pile & cash flow

JOYY is sitting on $3.3b net cash, including long-term deposits and fixed-income investments, its latest quarterly filing shows, while the company has a market cap below $2.3b as of May 22. Excluding goodwill and intangibles, net assets are about $4.3b, implying a 0.5x P/B ratio.

Revenue has declined slightly over the last three years, but operating cash flow has stabilized at around $300m annually. The company’s 2024 net loss was due to a one-off charge of goodwill impairment, which was non-cash and thus had no impact on cash flows.

Annual capex has averaged about $80m over the last three years, with only a small part being maintenance expenditure. Based on the OCF and capex trends, I expect free cash flow to equity to stay above $200m in the short to medium term.

Shareholder returns

Since the start of 2021, JOYY has spent about $1.1b repurchasing stock, reducing its share capital by more than a third. In March 2025, the company announced plans to pay $200m dividends annually over the next three years and to buy back shares for a total of $300m in the same period. Based on its current market cap, JOYY’s combined yield of dividends and buybacks would exceed 13% a year. Generous shareholder returns can be a key to unlocking value for companies trading at steep discounts.

It's worth noting that founder Li remains the top shareholder, with a 35% stake, and hence the biggest beneficiary of continuous dividends and buybacks. Lei Jun, chairman and CEO of Xiaomi Corp. (OTCPK:XIACF), is the No.2 shareholder with an interest of nearly 12%. As JOYY’s angel investor and ex-chairman, Lei has delegated his voting rights to Li.

Valuation

The DDM formula is often used to estimate intrinsic value based on the theory that a company is worth the sum of all its future dividends, discounted back to its present value. It’s appropriate for mature companies with stable dividend payments.

With zero growth assumed, dividends of $200m divided by an 8% cost of equity equal an intrinsic value of $2.5b for JOYY’s ongoing businesses, which adds to the company’s $3.3b net cash for a fair market cap of $5.8b. Applying a 20% margin of safety, I arrive at a price target of $89.71 per share. Alternatively, discounting an annual free cash flow to equity of about $200m would yield a similar result.

More background

JOYY built social media brands YY and Huya (HUYA) and eventually sold them with good timing and pricing. In two transactions in 2020 and 2023, the company sold Huya shares to internet giant Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) for a total of $1b. In another deal, JOYY struck an initial agreement with Baidu (BIDU) in late 2020, near the peak of a Chinese tech bubble, to sell YY for $3.6b. The deal was delayed by regulatory hurdles and ultimately revised to close in February this year with a reduced value of $2.1b. Both platforms were sold when they were about to run into development bottlenecks, and Li demonstrated his business acumen in timing these deals.

Key risks

JOYY relies on Bigo Live for a large part of its earnings, but the live-broadcasting platform’s user number and revenue have become stagnant. A decline in Bigo’s business would affect the sustainability of JOYY’s profit and cash flow. Nevertheless, I believe the Bigo risk could mainly hurt the stock’s upside, while the company’s ample cash reserve offers strong protection on the downside.

Secondly, Chinese tech stocks face the threat of forced delisting from US exchanges amid geopolitical tensions. Although JOYY is now based in Singapore with a diverse global revenue, it’s unclear whether the company would still be targeted for its Chinese origin and ties. I see delisting as a remote scenario and have applied a wide margin of safety to JOYY’s valuation. If it happens, it doesn’t mean the end of the world for long-term investors, as there could be relisting on an Asian exchange, probably the Hong Kong bourse.

Most of JOYY’s cash and equivalents are in USD, and the company derives the vast majority of its revenue from outside China. This would help ensure a smooth relisting process if necessary.

Conclusion

JOYY trades at a steep discount to its intrinsic value, partly due to negative sentiment toward Chinese tech names, as well as the company’s stagnant growth outlook. Generous shareholder returns should help unlock JOYY’s deep value, with a combined yield of dividends and buybacks exceeding 13% a year. I value the stock at more than twice its current market cap, based on discounted future dividends and a large cash pile. Key risks include Bigo’s potential downturn and the threat of forced delisting against US-listed Chinese tech stocks in general.

data.title: IonQ Isn't Anywhere Close To Nvidia
data.description:
IonQ, Inc. (NYSE:IONQ) has soared to new highs after the new CEO suggested the quantum computing company wanted to become like Nvidia (NVDA). Any company should clearly have lofty goals, but investors shouldn't value such a stock based on those goals. My investment thesis remains ultra-Bearish on IonQ after the big rally and likely coming up double top at $50.

Not Even IPO Nvidia

IonQ just reported Q1 2025 results, where revenues were just $7.6 million. The quantum computing company guided to revenue for the year of between $75 to $95 million.

The quantum computing company recently hired Niccolo de Masi as the CEO with the CEO Peter Chapman becoming Executive Chairman of the Board. The new CEO made the following statement to Barron's:

I believe IonQ will be the Nvidia player. There will be other people that copy us and follow us; they have always copied and followed us.

The new CEO's statement falls in line with the prior CEO making claims the quantum company will reach 2030 sales of $1 billion. The odd part of these statements is the prior CEO giving up the CEO role despite this supposed massive opportunity to grow sales from sub-$50 million in 2024 to the $1 billion goal.

As far as the Nvidia goal, the GPU company went public all the way back in 1999 at $12 per share. Nvidia went public with a focus on building GPUs for PC gaming and multimedia experiences, so the chip company naturally wasn't even focused on AI back when going public.

For IonQ to become Nvidia, the company will have to grow quantum computing sales for 20 years, then pivot to a new market where the company is the leader by far. Nvidia actually went public in 1999 and sales were around $500 million in 2000, so IonQ isn't even equal to where the GPU company went public 26 years ago.

Nvidia was quickly topping $1 billion sales by 2001. IonQ needs to become the quantum computing market leader with $1+ billion in sales before the market should take this goal as legitimate enough to reward the stock with a valuation topping $10 billion.

Focus On The Actual Targets

The current analyst estimates generally define what IonQ needs to accomplish to reach $1 billion in 2030 sales. The company will have to grow at a 70% annual clip through 2028 to just reach $500 million in sales.

Clearly, IonQ has one of the highest sales levels of the public companies, but D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) reported nearly double the sales in Q1 at $15 million. The bigger risk is that the mega tech companies all have quantum computing chips with impressive results. One has to really wonder whether one of these small public quantum computing companies is even the market leader in 5 years.

In addition, IonQ made several acquisitions likely to contribute to the revenue guidance for the year. Lightsynq Technologies makes quantum repeaters for networks, and Capella Space is focused on orbital sensors for space in what seems unrelated to the goals for quantum computing. IonQ is issuing 24.2 million shares for these firms costing a shocking combined $1.1 billion.

With the desire to invest more in the quantum revolution and these deals in new segments, IonQ quietly hiked the EBITDA loss guidance by 35% on the Q1 2025 earnings call as follows:

As Niccolo mentioned, we are making additional investments to accelerate our roadmap as well as entering new segments of the market and we expect to continue to invest in the ecosystems that support our customers.

Given our expansion, we are also prioritizing bringing acquired entities up to U.S. public company reporting standards. All of this means we expect to see an increase to our cost basis for the year. We envision this increasing our adjusted EBITDA loss previously projected $120 million by approximately 35%.

Oddly, the company only provided guidance in the Q1 2025 earnings release of matching the prior guidance for organic and inorganic revenues to reach $75 to $95 million in 2025, but the company didn't update the EBITDA loss guidance until the earnings call. IonQ is now targeting an adjusted EBITDA loss of $162 million for 2025.

The stock hitting all-time highs appears all hype, with the adjusted EBITDA loss boosted by $42 million for the year while revenues aren't rising from prior expectations. If anything, IonQ might only hit the revenue targets for 2025 due to the acquisitions.

The company had an adjusted EBITDA loss of $36 million for the quarter and burned $33 million in cash. At the current market cap of $11 billion with a cash balance of nearly $700 million, IonQ doesn't really have a lot of worries about cash burn with the ability to raise a lot of cash without diluting shareholders in a major way.

The bigger issue is that the stock trades at 135x 2025 sales targets. The market is already valuing the business as if it's the Nvidia of the quantum computing space.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that IonQ has a long way to go to even approach what Nvidia was when going public, much less that massive AI chip company now. Investors should not buy into the Nvidia hype, with the rally to $50 making the stock by far too expensive for a quantum computing company with limited actual results.

data.title: Apple Under Fire: The 25% Tariff Threat That Could Break The Stock
data.description:
Introduction

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has always been more than just a stock. It’s a legacy, it's a symbol of innovation, luxury, and resilience. I don’t know a person who didn’t want to own an Apple product at some point in the last 25 years.

But in 2025, the whole narrative about Apple is not the same. Investors are realizing that Apple’s premium valuation doesn’t hold up when taking into account the performance. Growth is slowing, and while the AI race is not lost yet, it’s being led by others. The Services aren’t exactly shining as well. And now, political pressure is threatening the foundation of Apple’s global supply chain. As of today, the company is facing the possibility of a 25% tariff on iPhones built outside the U.S. It doesn’t matter where they were built.

Today, I want to discuss Apple’s current financial state, recent developments, and the deeper risks that are forming due to tariffs and the fast-changing environment under the current administration. I will start with a quick recap of what has been going on lately in terms of results and performance. If you have been following the company in the past month or so, you can skip this part, there won’t be anything new for you.

Company Overview: Apple in 2025

Everyone knows Apple. But the company is not in the same state it was at its peak, and now the most important question is what has changed. The company still earns a lot. Apple is a printing machine for cash, with profitability metrics that most businesses can only dream of in terms of EBIT, EBITDA, ROCE, and ROA. But growth is the problem. EPS growth is negative. Revenue is flat, it’s almost not growing. Every forward-looking metric is weaker than Apple’s 5-year averages, unfortunately.

Apple’s once strongest and toughest Services segment is now going through legal problems (Epic, Google DOJ), threatening billions in App Store revenue. The hardware business, especially the iPhone, still drives demand, but we can already say that the direction is negative. Insiders have been selling for some time now, and this is one of the reasons investors have been getting nervous lately. And the AI, which could be Apple’s next big growth driver, seems unimpressive, with a lot of questions.

There’s also increasing tension around Apple’s manufacturing strategy. CEO Tim Cook has pushed for production diversification in India and Vietnam to mitigate geopolitical risk from China. But that strategy is now hitting a wall because one person is against it: Donald Trump.

Tariff Tensions

Today, President Donald Trump has made it clear that Apple’s global manufacturing strategy is not good enough. On May 23, he threatened to impose a 25% tariff on all iPhones made outside the United States, with an even broader 50% tariff proposal aimed at European Union goods if trade negotiations with the latter don’t accelerate. For Apple, which relies heavily on Asia for manufacturing, this is a direct threat to the core of its business.

To understand why this moment is so critical, we have to look at how Apple ended up here in the first place. Note: We will talk about the company right now, and not about the president, but of course, the president is the major reason for Apple’s tariff instabilities.

Over the past few years, Apple has smartly begun shifting large portions of its manufacturing out of China. That move was due to rising geopolitical tension, tariffs imposed during the original U.S.–China trade war, and COVID-19 lockdowns that exposed how fragile its supply chain really was. As a result, Apple slowly started to diversify. It moved a growing share of iPhone production to India, while Vietnam began assembling iPads, MacBooks, Apple Watches, and AirPods. It was a smart decision because both countries offered lower labor costs, great infrastructure, and more political neutrality, which was one of the main factors for Apple.

But as we see now, this calculated workaround, which took the company a lot of time and effort, doesn’t seem to align with the current administration’s vision about the future of U.S. companies, and specifically Apple’s.

A weird piece of news is that Trump’s message wasn’t vague. It was pointed at Apple and only at them. He directly called out Apple CEO Tim Cook, saying that he expects all iPhones sold in the U.S. to be built in America, not in India, not in China, not anywhere else. If that doesn’t happen, Trump insists Apple should pay a 25% tariff. All of us have dozens of questions right now, for example, how can he make it happen? And why would he slam tariffs only on one company? Is this even legal? I will answer them in a couple of minutes, but let’s roll with this possibility for a moment and understand what it means for Apple.

The biggest problem is that the company’s entire supply chain is built on scale and efficiency. Where the company assembles its products is very important, of course, but what is even more important is where the materials and components come from. I won’t even mention the professionals. You can’t replicate that in Ohio overnight. Hell, you can’t replicate that in Ohio ever. Sorry, but this is the hard truth. Bank of America said that building an iPhone in the U.S. would cost up to 90% more. I saw analyst estimations that only the cost for the company could be about $3500 per phone. The number is correct, that’s not a typo, in case you are wondering. What should the price of this phone be if it costs the company more than $3000 to produce? I have been using only Apple products for many years, you could say I am a fan because I seriously dislike Android and Windows, but even I won’t be buying an iPhone for $3000.

For the company, it’s completely economically unviable. In fact, it would take five to ten years of infrastructure development and a huge capex investment to even begin manufacturing iPhones domestically at scale. So, Trump’s demand, while dramatic, is impossible in the short term. Even if theoretically the company started this shift right now, they wouldn't be able to produce on the scale in the U.S. for longer than Trump will be in office. Right now, it looks like it will be cheaper for the company to take a 25% hit from tariffs and continue production outside the country. Let’s break down what other options the company has.

The first option is for Apple to try to digest the tariff and keep prices as they are, but that would hurt margins. Apple’s gross margin is lower compared to the sector even now. Imagine what will happen to it if the company decides to take a 25% hit on iPhones, which account for over half its revenue (the right answer is big trouble and the stock crash). Another option is to raise the price for consumers, but with interest rates high and consumer spending weakening, that’s a suicide. iPhones are already expensive, and even now, they are losing their market share slowly.

There’s also the chance that Apple will find some way to smooth things up. The diplomatic abilities of Tim Cook could help him find a workaround. For example, it can be some form of expanded U.S. investment, or maybe more AI data centers, chip partnerships, or some other efforts to ease political pressure and not damage Apple’s supply chain. So in theory, this could buy them some time, but it won’t eliminate all of the problems, and it would definitely not eliminate the risk.

The stock is already down more than 20% year to date. This drama around tariffs is adding even more uncertainty. An unstable economy is not the best time for purchasing expensive gadgets, so earnings are also a concern, but not the main one. Right now, the question is whether Apple’s business model, which relies on global manufacturing and low-cost production, can even survive a protectionist wave in one piece.

Can Apple defend itself without bending to political pressure? I am not sure, for Trump, all of the tariff matters seem like a very personal deal, so I don’t believe he will back off. If these tariffs on Apple somehow happen, this stock isn’t just overpriced, it’s structurally at risk, with a chance for a fix only after the next elections.

Could the tariffs happen?

The main question is, can Trump actually do it? Technically, yes. Under U.S. law, the president has broad authority to impose tariffs on national security grounds through Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act, or under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 if a country or a company’s actions are seen as unfair to U.S. trade interests. It doesn’t mean it would hold up in court forever, or that it wouldn’t be challenged, but the reality is that he can sign it and announce it. Trust me when I say, that in this environment, it will be enough to send the stock flying down double-digit percentage points.

Next, why Apple? Why just one company? The truth is, it’s not really about Apple, but Apple is a great candidate for Trump's point. Everyone gets what Apple does, it’s easy to explain to people. Apple is one of the most valuable companies in the world, and a giant of American tech. And it’s not producing at home. In Trump’s worldview, that’s a contradiction he’s aiming to correct. Apple becomes the perfect billboard for his “bring jobs home” narrative. Apple is the perfect example for others as well.

That brings us to the third question: Is it legal to target a single company like this? Not directly. The government can’t just write a law that says, “Apple must pay a tariff.” But what it can do is create a policy that applies to a category, for example, smartphones or electronics made outside the U.S., and Apple just happens to be the one it hits the hardest.

Conclusion

I’m not going to rate Apple right now. Honestly, I’m putting a Hold here just because I have to press something. The truth is, everything is still too fresh. Markets need time to process, and so do we. Maybe by Monday, the tone will calm down, the headlines will shift, and we’ll start hearing about some sort of compromise. But as of today, this doesn’t look good. I still love the company, and I still use the products, but as an investor, I’m staying very cautious. (I am not a shareholder, by the way.)

data.title: Accenture: 2024 Replay
data.description:
ACN Stock: Previous thesis and new developments

On June 6, 2024, I initiated coverage on Accenture plc (NYSE:ACN) stock. As you can see from the following screenshot, that article was titled "Accenture: Buy The 25% Dip." I argued for a Buy rating because I believe the price dip at that time reflected a market overaction. Quote:

Accenture's stock prices have corrected by more than 25% recently, presenting a buying opportunity for potential investors. Analysts project a compound annual growth rate of 12.6% for Accenture's EPS over the next five years. The projection is well-supported by strong earnings catalysts like digital transformation, AI, and external acquisitions.

At the time of publication, ACN was priced at ~$291. The stock price has quickly staged a robust rally and peaked around $400 per share in early 2025, as seen below.

Then a few recent developments have triggered another sizable correction, which triggered this follow-up coverage on the stock. In the rest of this article, I will focus on the top 2 developments on my list: A) the changes in the macroeconomic environments, and B) also its business operations as updated in its FQ2 earnings report (ER) announced recently. In the end, my assessment is that the market is once again overacting to temporary headwinds. The significant valuation correction started in early 2025 has presented another compelling buying opportunity for potential investors.

Let me start with the FQ2 ER as a primer. ACN announced the ER earnings on March 20, 2025, with a set of robust results as seen in the chart below. For this quarter, both the normalized and GAAP EPS came in at $2.82, and both beat the estimates. Total revenue reached $16.66 billion and also surpassed estimated. Looking ahead, ACN is projected to announce its upcoming quarter's earnings on June 20, 2025, with a normalized EPS estimate of $3.31 and total revenue of $17.26 billion, translating into a healthy sequential growth of about 17% and 4%, respectively. As to be detailed later, I see plenty of catalysts that could drive such growth based on the business updates provided in the ER.

ACN Stock: A wonderful business at a discounted price

However, due to the mixed macroeconomic environment (especially after the tariff disputes started in early April), ACN suffered large price corrections and valuation compression despite the above up-beating results. For example, the chart below shows that Accenture's current P/E (on a Non-GAAP and TTM basis) to be ~21.2x, which is below its 5-year average of 30.32 by a sizable margin (of more than 15%). Other P/E metrics (such as Non-GAAP and FWD ratios) show as similar discount.

Admittedly, ACN’s current valuation is still above the sector median by some metrics. For example, its non-GAAP TTM P/E is more than 20% higher than the sector median of 21.26. However, I do not think the sector median is a good benchmark for ACN due to its competitive moats.

ACN boasts unparalleled global scale and industry breadth, which allows it to tackle massive, complex projects that most consulting firms in the sector cannot. As a reflection of this moat, ACN enjoys far superior profitability than the sector average. As you can see from the following table, the company receives an overall A+ profitability grade for good reasons. Its EBITDA Margin (TTM) of 17.07% is 65% above the sector median. Walking down the income statement, the differentiation becomes more pronounced. Its EBIT Margin (TTM) of 15.32% is a substantial above the sector median of 5.96% (by more than 157%), and the net profit margin is more than 2x of the sector median.

ACN’s AI Potential

Additionally, the current valuation also discounted its growth potential, especially on the AI front. Judging by the updates provided in the ER, management has been aggressively investing in long-term revenue growth opportunities, with a particular focus on AI-related opportunities.

The company reported significant and ongoing investment in research and development efforts in these areas, as well as strategic acquisitions (which is another key differentiator that sets it apart from peers in my view). The company has expedited employee training in preparation for generative AI trends. Many recent acquisitions are targeted in the areas of AI technology and also an expansion of its financial offerings. At the same time, Accenture has also established a collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA) to develop an intelligent system, which aims at rendering a digital reality of warehouses to enable automation opportunities through AI training and robotics.

These internal and acquisition activities are reflected in (and also well-supported by) its cash deployment, as seen from its latest cash flow statement below. As seen, the company has been consistently investing in its CAPEX and even more aggressively on cash acquisitions. Take the latest quarter as an example, the cash from operations was over $2,853 million as seen. Capital expenditure was about 170 million, and cash acquisitions were around $307 million.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Before closing, a few additional risks, both in the upside and downside directions, are worth mentioning. The company has a long track record of paying dividends, and the current yield is significantly above average. To wit, the chart below shows ACN’s dividend yield in the past 5 years. As seen, it has fluctuated with a low point of 0.87% and a peak of 2.05% in the past 5 years. However, most of the time, the yield stayed within a tractable range of around 1% and 1.6%, illustrating the stability of the stock. The average dividend yield is 1.37% in this period. Currently, the yield stands at 1.81%, thus not only far above the historical average but also among the most attractive levels in at least the past 5 years.

To echo and further elaborate on a point mentioned earlier, the mixed macroeconomic environment is a key downside risk. ACN faces largely the same set of generic risks as of other IT consulting firms, such as risks of cyberattacks, data breaches, etc. However, compared to many other firms in the sector, ACN relies more on large-scale government contracts, thus making it more sensitive to potential shifts in administrative priorities. For example, the DOGE (the Department of Government Efficiency) initiatives could lead to reduced numbers of government contracts for ACN and/or increased scrutiny of existing contracts. Judging by the results in the ER, I also feel that securing small contracts has proven to be a challenge too. The ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties are most likely the root cause here too, as broad-market uncertainties (such as trade disputes) tend to lower customer expenditure budgets. But for the long term, I believe these issues are ultimately temporary.

To close, this analysis of ACN is mainly triggered by two new developments since my last writing: the changes in the macroeconomics and its FQ2 earnings report. My verdict is that the market has once again overacted to temporary issues. Once looking past these speed bumps, I see a highly profitable business with promising growth potential (especially AI-related) for sale at a discounted valuation, leading to another buy recommendation.

data.title: UPS: The Near 7% Yield Is Worth A Look, Shares Near Key Support
data.description:
UPS (NYSE:UPS) ranks No. 11 in the S&P 500 in terms of dividend yield. The forward rate is now 6.84%, according to Seeking Alpha's data as of May 21, 2025. I assert the dividend will hold, offering investors an opportunity to own a blue-chip company at a favorable valuation today.

To be clear, I was bullish on UPS two years ago, and shares have performed terribly since then. It's down 40% since June 29, 2023, sharply underperforming the S&P 500, which has been up 33%.

UPS has battled FedEx (FDX) for years on end, and recent news that it's losing service with Amazon (AMZN) wasn't exactly a bullish sign. Still, I reiterate a buy rating and will update my valuation and provide a fresh view on the technicals.

S&P 500 Biggest Dividend Yields: UPS No. 11

Back in April, UPS reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $1.49 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast of $1.38, while revenue of $21.5 billion, down 1% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $480 million beat. Shares dipped 0.4% in the session that followed, however, as the $81 billion market cap transportation company bypassed giving full-year operating guidance due to tariff uncertainty.

On the call, the management team said it was ramping up its cost-cutting efforts as operational challenges persisted. Both industry-specific and macroeconomic uncertainties clouded the forward view, while the end of the de minimis exemption was a specific bearish catalyst.

In a major shake-up, UPS intends to slash $3.5 billion in costs this year and 20,000 positions. Fifty percent of its Amazon-related sales will likely go away by the middle of 2026-the execs framed that as a positive, since 60% of revenue there is loss-making.

But it was actually a decent start to the year. Its domestic business drove the solid performance, achieving an operating margin of 7.0%, well above the street consensus. While challenges are many, this could be UPS's year of efficiency-if so, the low-teens earnings multiple is a bargain. I was concerned about the company citing weaker sales as Q1 progressed-that will be something to watch out for in the next quarterly report.

On the earnings outlook, we should see some stability after the current year's negative non-GAAP EPS change. The out-year is forecast to earn $8 in EPS, while FY 2027 bottom-line growth could be in the mid- to high-single digits.

Of course, there have been many sell-side earnings downgrades in the past 90 days, with zero upgrades. Still, profitability trends are very strong, highlighted by free cash flow per share of $6.30, resulting in a FCF yield of 6.6%, which buttresses the case that the high 6.8% dividend yield is safe.

UPS: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have significant changes to my stale 2023 price target. If we assume $7.40 of operating EPS over the next 12 months and apply a 15x multiple (below the long-term average of 17.3x), then shares should trade near $111, making the stock undervalued.

I would lead toward a higher P/E considering that this cyclical stock is potentially near trough earnings, which would warrant an above-average multiple. On an upside-case basis, a 20x P/E would yield a $148 fair value. The price-to-sales ratio is severely depressed, which would align with the loftier price-target scenario.

For now, though, I'll stay with the conservative view.

UPS: Very Cheap On Earnings & Sales, Big Yield

UPS Yield Close to 7%, Historically High

Key risks include high uncertainty regarding its cost-cutting efforts, with potential downside risks to its network configuration. Rising costs, trade-war uncertainties, and short-run profitability are downsides to look out for, too. Moreover, further weakness among US small- and medium-sized businesses would further hurt sales growth.

I can't ignore the risk of a dividend cut if conditions deteriorate, but I believe it's safe given free cash flow levels. Low fuel prices are a current boon, but higher oil prices would ding margins.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows an unconfirmed Q2 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, July 29 BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

UPS's chart offers hope for long-time shareholders. Notice in the graph below that the stock is closing in on a key support area around $80. While that's about 15% more potential downside, it sets up for a favorable risk/reward if we see a long-term positive inflection. I also see that there's a downtrend support line that comes into play in the low $80s, offering confluence to the horizontal buying point drawn.

But take a look at the 40-week moving average (which translates to the 200-day moving average)-it's negatively sloped, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. Also, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart remains in bearish territory, confirming the lower move in the stock. Resistance is seen between $133 and $136.

UPS: Long-Term Downtrend Nears Support

The Bottom Line

With technical support in view and shares depressed on valuation, I have a buy rating on UPS. Now almost a 7%-yielder, the embattled Industrials stock features a solid risk/reward in my assessment.

data.title: Will Gold Break Its All-Time High In 2025? Momentum Builds Ahead Of Key Level
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Gold has been back on its horses towards the second part of the month after retracing 10.8% from its April highs.Safe-Haven assets are bid today as President Trump reignites tariff discussions.Trading at $3,346, the precious metal is less than $155 (3.80%) from its all-time highs.Technical Analysis update for the Bullion.

XAU/USD Technical Analysis

Gold has rebounded strongly from its monthly lows, marked at $3,120, trading $200 dollars higher in the past week.The precious metal found some relief from last week’s pullback, as easing tariff concerns shifted market focus toward the recent U.S. credit downgrade, triggering a fresh wave of buying interest.XAU/USD is now at the highs of its Daily Descending Channel, though the MA 20 and 200 are not far below and acting as immediate support.The end of session will be key to spot if the appetite for Safe-Haven assets is strong going into the week-end. Potential triggers are explained in Wednesday's gold update.The RSI is close to overbought with a 4H bearish divergence, which has yet to be confirmed.

Technical Levels

We are eyeing at immediate resistance with prices approaching the highs of the daily channel.A rejection of today's highs near the $3,350 psychological level may hint at a return towards the lows of the channel, though key Moving Averages will first have to be broken.A return to the lows of the channel will be aiming at the $3,100 level.

  • Support 1 : 3,270 - 3,290
  • Support 2: 3,200 - 3,225
  • Support 3: 3,100 Channel Lows
  • Support 4: 3,052
  • Resistance 1: 3,414 - 3,436
  • Resistance 2: $3,500.20 Recent All-time Highs
  • Resistance 3: 3,550 Psychological Level
  • Resistance 4: 3,598 - 3,600

Trader's Positioning

Trader's positioning is key to see if an extreme is attained, where one may expect reversalsTraders are about 70% Long vs 30% Short, which may indicate more downside for the precious metal - although, positioning is not at extremes.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Gold And S&P 500 Both Volatile
data.description:
It’s been a volatile year for stocks so far, but more surprising is that gold prices, which have rallied more than 25% YTD, have been even more volatile. Year to date, the SPDR Gold Trust (GLD) has experienced a daily move of at least 1% on 40% of all trading days compared to 37% for the S&P 500, as measured by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

The chart below shows the rolling 50-day average number of daily 1% moves in GLD over the last 20 years. The current level of 54% is modestly below the recent high of 56%, and that was the highest frequency of daily 1% moves since late 2011! The second chart shows the same calculation for SPY over the last 20 years. While the current level of 42% is hardly extreme relative to history, earlier this month it was at 52%.

In both charts, areas where the line shifts from blue to red indicate periods when both GLD and SPY had 1%+ daily moves on more than 40% of trading days in a rolling 50-day period. The only periods in the last 20 years when both ETFs simultaneously had 1%+ daily moves over 50 days were during the Financial Crisis, late 2011 after S&P downgraded the US AAA credit rating, early 2016, Covid, and now.

The chart below shows the performance of GLD over the last 20 years, and here again, the red line indicates the periods when both GLD and SPY had 1%+ daily moves at least 40% of the time over a 50-day span. For GLD, there was no consistent pattern of performance leading up to or after these periods.

For the S&P 500, however, the pattern surrounding these occurrences was more consistent. Except the Financial crisis when elevated levels of volatility in both ETFs started just after the S&P 500’s peak and continued for most of the bear market, in the three other periods (and the current period), it wasn’t until late in the decline or after the market made its low that the average number of 1% daily moves in each ETF exceeded 40%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
